title: Observations on China's Society and Global Strategy
content:
In recent years, the intense political crackdowns in China have fundamentally altered the nation's societal fabric and governance. Sweeping anti-corruption campaigns were often driven by the leadership's realization that traditional channels of information had been captured by unreliable proxies. By striking under the guise of anti-corruption, the central government sought to regain absolute control over its massive administrative bureaucracy. However, this relentless political maneuvering has inadvertently led to a profound shift in personal pursuits and societal values. For the first time in the history of the modern republic, the broader society feels increasingly stagnant rather than exalted by an economic boom.

During the heyday of capitalist reform, a distinct herd mentality heavily dominated public thinking across the country. Enormous amounts of money and energy poured into hot sectors like real estate and technology, creating massive economic bubbles that eventually burst. Internalizing these painful economic lessons has led younger generations to adopt a remarkably different kind of modern individualism. As they recover from the exhausting toll of extreme competition, more citizens are quietly letting go of strict societal expectations. They are increasingly choosing to follow their personal passions rather than blindly participating in the chaotic pursuit of endless wealth.

This domestic stagnation has significantly impacted the nation's ability to remain globally attractive to foreign students, skilled migrants, and international investors. In contrast, the United States continues to hold built-in advantages regarding global talent acquisition and cultural integration. America boasts a relatively open culture, elite educational institutions, and a long history of successfully integrating newcomers into its society. These immigrants often forge invaluable institutional links between their new home and their countries of origin. Throwing away these long-established strengths would be a tragic mistake that could deeply compromise America's standing in the modern global economy.
